stem: Which feedback actually helps Claude fix an extraction defect?
options: "It's not handling edge cases well — be more careful"
"The output quality has been declining lately"
"For input X, the expected key `service_visits` is missing because the source says 'maintenance entries' instead — here's the excerpt and the rule violated"
"Try again, but better"
rationales: Nothing to locate, nothing to act on.
A trend report without a single failing example.
Concrete and executable: specific input, expected vs actual, the trigger, the rule. The model can fix exactly that — and the fix generalizes to the pattern.
A retry request, not feedback.

stem: Requirements for a caching layer are genuinely uncertain — consistency needs, invalidation, TTLs are all fuzzy. What should Claude do before implementing?
options: Interview the user — surface the decisions and trade-offs that must be resolved before code is written
Implement the most common caching pattern and iterate
Implement all plausible variants behind feature flags
Wait for a complete written specification
rationales: For caching, real-time architecture, auth, and data consistency, wrong assumptions are expensive to unwind. Asking Claude to surface decisions first turns hidden assumptions into explicit choices.
Common patterns encode assumptions the user hasn't agreed to — precisely the risk.
Speculative variants multiply cost and complexity before understanding exists.
Passive waiting wastes the model's ability to elicit the requirements.

stem: The same defect keeps recurring across many runs of the same prompt. What does this signal?
options: The model needs more retries with slight temperature variation
The task exceeds the model's capability
Random noise — occasional defects are expected
The prompt or schema needs a structural change — a few-shot example, a split tool, a new field. Prompt-level fixes generalize; per-instance retries do not
rationales: Retries re-sample the same flawed setup.
Recurrence under one prompt indicts the prompt before the model.
Recurring is the opposite of random — a stable pattern is a signal.
Repetition means the failure is structural. Change the structure once and every future run benefits.

stem: Generated tests keep asserting only that functions don't throw, duplicating coverage, and ignoring project fixtures. What is the fix?
options: Generate more tests so the good ones outnumber the bad
Document test standards in project memory or a testing guide — examples of valuable behavioral tests vs trivial ones, fixture names and intended use
Have a second model filter the weak tests
Stop generating tests; write them manually
rationales: Volume multiplies the pattern, not the quality.
The model produces low-value tests because it doesn't know what this project values. Standards with contrasting examples and fixture documentation teach it — durably, for every future session.
A filter model needs the same standards defined anyway; define them once where generation can use them.
Abandoning generation forfeits the value that better guidance would recover.

stem: A code review agent is noisy and inconsistent. What does it need most?
options: A larger model tier
Permission to auto-fix what it finds
Longer context so it can read the whole codebase
Explicit report criteria: which findings matter (bugs, security, data loss, missing tests, breaking API changes) and what to skip (style nits, accepted conventions, speculative performance advice)
rationales: Capability without criteria produces confident noise.
Auto-fixing amplifies the noise problem into a change-control problem.
More visibility without a definition of "reportable" means more findings, not better ones.
A review agent is only as good as its definition of signal. Explicit include/skip criteria is the foundation everything else builds on.

stem: To reduce a review agent's false positives, which intervention works best?
options: Few-shot examples showing acceptable code patterns side-by-side with genuinely problematic ones
Adding "be conservative and only report real issues" to the prompt
Lowering the maximum findings count per review
Running the review twice and reporting only overlapping findings
rationales: The agent's problem is a fuzzy boundary between acceptable and problematic. Contrasting examples draw the line concretely — the reliably effective fix.
"Be conservative" is vague instruction against a boundary problem; the model doesn't know where your line is.
A cap truncates output without improving judgment — real issues may be what gets cut.
Consensus filters random noise, not systematic misjudgment — both runs share the same fuzzy boundary.

stem: Developers dismiss 35% of a review agent's findings, but the team can't tell what to fix. What fields make the dismissals actionable?
options: Reviewer name and timestamp
The full diff for each dismissed finding
`detected_pattern`, `rule_id`, and `evidence` — so dismiss rates can be aggregated by pattern and the over-reporting patterns can be suppressed in the prompt criteria
A severity score from 1 to 10
rationales: Who dismissed when doesn't reveal what construct triggered the noise.
Raw diffs require manual re-analysis of every dismissal.
Without pattern-level fields you can only see "35% dismissed," not which kinds of findings to suppress. With them, dismissals become a targeted improvement loop.
Severity doesn't explain why findings were judged wrong.

stem: Along which dimensions should an extraction pipeline's evaluation results be segmented?
options: By model version only — everything else is noise
Document type, field, prompt version, model, source quality, confidence band, and reviewer correction category
By calendar week
By reviewer, to identify strict graders
rationales: Model version is one axis of many — failures cluster along the others too.
Aggregate accuracy misleads; segmentation reveals that "97% overall" hides a failing field or document type. These axes localize where quality actually breaks.
Time matters only when something changed — the listed dimensions explain why.
Reviewer variance is worth knowing but doesn't localize pipeline failures.

stem: One test fails after a change. What should you ask Claude for?
options: A targeted fix, providing the failing test name and its assertion message
A full rewrite of the module — a clean slate avoids compounding errors
An explanation of the module's design first
Deletion of the failing test if the code looks right
rationales: Narrow failure, narrow fix. The exact failing test with its assertion is executable feedback pointing at the defect.
Rewrites after narrow failures introduce new regressions across everything that worked.
Sometimes useful context — but the direct path is the failing test.
Deleting the signal because the code "looks right" is how bugs ship.
sourceRef: ## 11 > Common Pitfalls (full rewrite after narrow failure)

stem: A team sees repeated extraction failures and immediately starts building a custom validation microservice with a human-review dashboard. What pitfall is this?
options: Under-investing — they should also build a retraining pipeline
Choosing the wrong cloud provider for the dashboard
Nothing — infrastructure is always the right response to quality issues

Adding infrastructure before improving examples and criteria — repeated failure patterns are usually solved by prompt/schema changes (few-shot examples, better absence semantics), which are cheaper and generalize
rationales: More infrastructure on top of unexamined failure patterns compounds the mistake.
The provider isn't the problem; the sequencing is.
Infrastructure has its place — after the cheap, high-leverage fixes are exhausted.
The guide's ordering: prompt and schema improvements first; heavier interventions only if the metric doesn't move. Most recurring patterns die at step one.
